title: What an Agent Skill Is
objective: Explain what an Agent Skill is, how it differs from a prompt, the two required metadata fields, and when to build one.

sources.title: Agent Skills — Overview (Claude Platform Docs)
sources.trust_rationale: Anthropic's canonical product documentation for Agent Skills.

sources.title: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills (Anthropic Engineering)
sources.trust_rationale: Anthropic's own engineering blog explaining the design and rationale.

sources.title: anthropics/skills — README + template (GitHub)
sources.trust_rationale: Anthropic's official Skills repository and minimal template.

sources.title: Extend Claude with skills (Claude Code Docs)

beats.narration: An Agent Skill is, at its simplest, a directory containing a file called SKILL.md. That file holds instructions, metadata, and optionally scripts and resources that Claude discovers and loads when they're relevant. The useful mental model, from Anthropic's own team: building a skill is like writing an onboarding guide for a new hire.

visual.alt: A folder named pdf containing SKILL.md and supporting files, feeding into a specialised Claude

beats.narration: So how is that different from just prompting well? A prompt is a conversation-level instruction for a one-off task. A skill is a reusable, filesystem-based resource that loads on demand. Instead of re-pasting the same guidance every time, you capture it once, and Claude pulls it in automatically whenever a task matches.

visual.alt: Side-by-side comparison of a prompt versus a skill

beats.narration: The format is deliberately tiny. A SKILL.md must begin with YAML frontmatter carrying just two required fields: a name, and a description. Everything below the frontmatter is plain Markdown — the instructions Claude follows when the skill is active. That's the entire required contract. The simplicity is the point.

visual.alt: A minimal SKILL.md showing name and description frontmatter above a Markdown body

beats.prompt: Which two fields are required in a SKILL.md's frontmatter?
beats.correct_feedback: Correct. Only name and description are required; everything else is optional.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. The two required frontmatter fields are name and description — everything else, like version or author, is optional.

beats.narration: Why does this shape matter? Because skills compose. Rather than building a separate custom agent for every use case, you give one general agent a stack of skills — a PDF skill, a brand skill, a database skill — and it becomes a specialist exactly where you need it. Your procedural knowledge turns into shareable, composable capability.

visual.alt: A general agent plus several skills combining into a specialised agent

beats.narration: A practical trigger for creating one: you notice you keep pasting the same instructions or checklist into chat, or a multi-step procedure keeps recurring, or a section of your CLAUDE.md has quietly become a procedure rather than a fact. That repetition is the signal. And because a skill's body loads only when used, long reference material costs almost nothing until it's needed.

visual.alt: A checklist of signals that it is time to build a skill

beats.prompt: What best captures how a skill differs from a prompt?

options.text: A skill is reusable and loads on demand; a prompt is one-off
A skill can only ever contain a single line of text
A prompt can bundle scripts, but a skill cannot

beats.correct_feedback: Right. Skills are reusable, filesystem-based, and load on demand, unlike one-off prompts.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. A skill is reusable and loads on demand, whereas a prompt is a one-off, conversation-level instruction.
